metadata.summary: Purchase Reserved Instances, Savings Plans, and Spot Instances to cut committed baseline spend by 30-72% and variable/batch spend by 60-90%. Requires 30+ days of stable usage data.

content.title: Cloud Commitment Discounts: RIs, Savings Plans, and Spot
content.summary: Three commitment discount mechanisms cover different workload shapes: Reserved Instances (RI) for stable instance-family workloads (up to 75% savings), Savings Plans for flexible compute baseline (up to 72%), and Spot Instances for fault-tolerant batch/ML (70-90%). Apply in that order after rightsizing; committing to oversized instances wastes both compute and discount opportunity.

why.p: Cloud providers price On-Demand instances to cover their capex with no commitment. Customers who commit to minimum usage receive the provider's volume discount — effectively paying wholesale instead of retail. The trade-off is flexibility: you pay for committed hours whether or not you use them.

The risk profile differs by mechanism: Savings Plans are the most flexible (compute-family, not instance-specific), Standard RIs are the least flexible (locked to instance family and AZ), Spot is fully flexible but interruptible. A typical optimization stack: 60-80% of baseline covered by Savings Plans/RIs, burst by On-Demand, batch/ML by Spot.

list.item: 30+ days of stable workload data available — required to identify baseline vs variable usage before committing.
Baseline compute represents more than $5k/month On-Demand — below that, Savings Plan overhead outweighs savings.
Batch jobs, CI/CD runners, or ML training that tolerate interruption — these are Spot candidates (70-90% savings).
After rightsizing — commit to the rightsized baseline, never the over-provisioned original.
Less than 30 days of usage data — commit to wrong baseline and you overpay or underbuy.
Workloads under active architectural rethink — a refactor may eliminate the service entirely; committed RIs become stranded.
Highly variable workloads with no stable floor — Savings Plans need a minimum hourly floor; if hourly spend varies 10x, even a conservative floor may underutilize.

section.name: savings-plans
section.p: AWS Compute Savings Plans cover any EC2, Fargate, or Lambda usage in any region. EC2 Instance Savings Plans cover a specific instance family in a region at a deeper discount. Start with Compute SPs for flexibility; add EC2 Instance SPs once instance family stabilizes.

rule.statement: Before purchasing Savings Plans, calculate the minimum hourly On-Demand compute spend over the previous 90 days (use the 5th percentile, not the average). Commit to 50-60% of that minimum as the first Compute Savings Plan. Increase coverage quarterly by reviewing utilization reports — target 70-80% baseline coverage, never exceed 85%.
rule.rationale: The 5th percentile floor is the amount spent in the cheapest hours of the last 90 days — the safest commitment. Starting at 50-60% coverage leaves buffer for workload reduction. Exceeding 85% coverage risks underutilization if business scales down.

example.title: SaaS startup: 3-phase commitment ramp
example.code:
Month 1: Conservative Compute Savings Plans Hourly commitment: $20/hour ($14,600/month) Coverage: 48% of average spend Risk: Low (well under minimum baseline) Savings: $5,256/month (36% on covered)
Month 3: Add EC2 Instance Savings Plans Additional commitment: $10/hour ($7,300/month) Total coverage: 72% of baseline Combined savings: $8,760/month
Month 4: Spot for batch jobs Migrated: background jobs, data processing Spot usage: $3,000/month (was $10,000 On-Demand) Savings: $7,000/month
Final: $50k On-Demand → $32,900/month total (-34%)
example.note: Ramp-up over 3-4 months avoids over-committing before usage patterns stabilize.

example.title: Over-committing based on peak spend
example.code:
Mistake: Committed $50/hour based on average monthly spend Reality: Minimum hourly spend (5th percentile) was $28/hour
Result: 35% of committed Savings Plan hours unused = wasted Annual wasted commitment: $94,608
example.note: Use 5th percentile of hourly spend, not monthly average divided by hours.

section.name: reserved-instances
section.p: Reserved Instances lock to a specific instance family (Standard) or allow instance-size flexibility within a family (Convertible). Standard RIs give up to 75% discount; Convertible up to 66%. Use Standard when the instance family is stable; Convertible when architecture may change.

rule.statement: Analyze 3+ months of stable workload data before purchasing RIs. Calculate RI coverage target as 60-80% of baseline (minimum steady-state) instance count. Choose Convertible RIs when the instance family may change within the commitment term; Standard when locked to a specific family. Monitor RI utilization weekly — underutilization below 80% indicates over-purchase.
rule.rationale: RI underutilization wastes the discount premium. Convertible RIs cost slightly more but allow exchanges — worth the premium during architectural evolution.

example.title: E-commerce RI purchase after rightsizing
example.code:
Analysis: 6 months of production data post-rightsizing Baseline: 30 steady-state m5.large instances Variable: 10-20 additional during peak
RI purchase: 24 x 3-year Convertible m5.large (80% of min baseline) On-demand: remaining 6 baseline + all variable burst RI discount: 35% on covered instances Monthly savings: $28,000/month on covered compute
example.note: Only purchase RIs after rightsizing. Committing to the old m5.2xlarge would have locked waste at a discount.

section.name: spot-instances
section.p: Spot instances are spare EC2/GCE/Azure Spot capacity offered at 70-90% discount. The trade-off: the provider can reclaim them with 2-minute notice. Eligible workloads must checkpoint state or be inherently stateless/restartable.

rule.statement: Use Spot instances for: batch processing, CI/CD runners, ML training jobs (with checkpointing every 30 minutes), and stateless web services behind a load balancer. Spot MUST NOT be used for: stateful databases, coordination services (ZooKeeper, etcd), or any service without automated restart handling. Always configure multi-AZ diversification with at least 3 instance type pools to reduce interruption probability.
rule.rationale: Diversification across instance types and AZs reduces Spot interruption rate from ~5% to under 1% — the provider rarely reclaims capacity across all pools simultaneously.

example.title: ML training: $191k/month savings via Spot + checkpointing
example.code:
Before: 20 concurrent p3.8xlarge On-Demand ($12.24/hr each) Monthly GPU hours: 19,200 Monthly cost: $235,008
Optimization 1 - Spot with checkpointing: Spot price: ~$3.67/hr (70% discount) Interruption rate: ~5% Extra training time from restarts: +10%
Optimization 2 - GPU rightsizing: Small models: g4dn.xlarge ($0.526/hr Spot) Medium models: p3.2xlarge ($3.06/hr Spot) Large models: p3.8xlarge (Spot only)
Optimization 3 - Mixed precision (torch.cuda.amp): Training speedup: 2x GPU hours needed: 50% reduction
Result: 19,200 GPU hours → 10,560 hours Average cost: $12.24/hr → $4.12/hr Monthly spend: $235,008 → $43,507 Savings: $191,501/month (81%)
example.note: Checkpointing every 30 minutes caps restart overhead at 30 minutes per interruption. Mixed precision doubles throughput before Spot is even applied.

example.title: Spot interruption handling pattern
example.code:
# Checkpoint every 30 minutes for ML training checkpoint_callback = ModelCheckpoint( dirpath='s3://checkpoints/', save_top_k=3, every_n_train_steps=1000 )
# Handle Spot interruption notice (2-min warning) def on_termination_notice(): save_checkpoint() log_progress_to_dynamodb()
example.note: AWS sends termination notice via instance metadata 2 minutes before reclaim. Poll http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/spot/termination-time every 5 seconds.

antipattern.description: Running stateful services (PostgreSQL, Redis, Kafka) on Spot instances without a promotion-to-On-Demand fallback.
antipattern.reason: 2-minute termination notice is insufficient for graceful database shutdown and data flush to storage. Use On-Demand or Reserved for stateful services; Spot only for stateless or checkpointed workloads.

antipattern.description: Using a single Spot pool (one instance type, one AZ) for a Spot fleet.
antipattern.reason: When the provider reclaims that pool, 100% of the fleet is interrupted simultaneously. Diversify across 3+ instance types and 2+ AZs to reduce fleet-wide interruption probability below 1%.

rule.statement: For multi-cloud environments, purchase provider-native commitment instruments per cloud rather than relying on third-party aggregators. Track commitment coverage quarterly via a coverage report: total spend, committed portion (RI/SP/CUD), On-Demand portion, Spot portion, and utilization of purchased commitments. Alert when RI/SP utilization drops below 80%.
rule.rationale: Commitment utilization below 80% means you are paying for reserved capacity you do not use — the inverse of the intended discount. Quarterly review catches underutilization before the commitment renews.

checklist.check: Collect 30+ days of stable usage data before any commitment purchase.
Rightsize instances before committing — never RI an oversized instance.
Calculate 5th-percentile hourly spend for Savings Plan commitment floor.
Start at 50-60% baseline coverage; ramp quarterly to 70-80%.
Choose Convertible RIs when instance family may change; Standard when stable.
Identify Spot-eligible workloads: batch, CI/CD, ML training, stateless web.
Implement Spot interruption handling: checkpointing, graceful shutdown scripts, multi-AZ diversification.
Set up Spot Fleet or managed Spot groups with 3+ instance type pools.
Configure fallback to On-Demand for Spot fleets.
Monitor RI/SP utilization weekly — alert below 80%.
Review commitments quarterly for coverage adjustments and upcoming expirations.
